search_results.title: miRTex: A Text Mining System for miRNA-Gene Relation Extraction
search_results.snippet: In this paper, we describe miRTex, a text mining system that extracts miRNA-target relations, as well as miRNA-gene and gene-miRNA regulation relations.

message.content:
Figure~\ref{fig:time-series-ina} shows the time course of the fast sodium current ($I_\mathrm{Na}$) during a simulated action potential under quinidine exposure at the median concentration of 12948 nM. The current exhibits a characteristic rapid inward peak during the upstroke phase around 11 ms, reaching approximately -0.84 nA/$\mu$F, followed by rapid inactivation. Compared to control conditions (0 nM), where the peak is more pronounced at around -2.04 nA/$\mu$F near 13 ms, quinidine substantially reduces the peak amplitude and accelerates inactivation, consistent with its known open-channel block mechanism. The median maximum upstroke velocity ($dV/dt_\mathrm{max}$) is reduced to 119.5 V/s (95\% CI: 109.7--126.4 V/s) from typical control values, reflecting $I_\mathrm{Na}$ inhibition, with no depolarization failure at this concentration across simulations.

Figure~\ref{fig:time-series-ical} displays the L-type calcium current ($I_\mathrm{CaL}$) over time, highlighting its activation during the plateau phase. At 12948 nM quinidine, $I_\mathrm{CaL}$ activates around 14 ms, peaking at approximately -3171 nA/$\mu$F, with a prolonged time course and reduced peak magnitude compared to control, where the peak is less negative but broader. The area under the curve (AUC) for $I_\mathrm{CaL}$ is markedly decreased to a median of -432 $\mu$C/$\mu$F (95\% CI: -437 to -429 $\mu$C/$\mu$F), indicating significant block, which contributes to APD prolongation (median APD90 of 524 ms, 95\% CI: 494--555 ms). This reflects quinidine's moderate inhibition of L-type channels, altering calcium-dependent repolarization dynamics.

Figure~\ref{fig:time-series-iks} illustrates the slow delayed rectifier potassium current ($I_\mathrm{Ks}$) throughout the action potential. Under quinidine at 12948 nM, $I_\mathrm{Ks}$ shows gradual activation starting around 20 ms, with a slow increase to approximately 3.8 nA/$\mu$F by late repolarization (around 500 ms), contrasting with control where it rises more modestly to about 0.0005 nA/$\mu$F initially but accumulates less overall. The time-dependent accumulation is enhanced under drug, yet repolarization is delayed due to dominant effects on other currents, aligning with median CaTD90 of 475 ms (95\% CI: 434--488 ms). Quinidine's weak direct effect on $I_\mathrm{Ks}$ is evident, with current trajectories supporting prolonged plateau duration.

Figure~\ref{fig:time-series-ik1} depicts the inward rectifier potassium current ($I_\mathrm{K1}$) time series, dominant during diastolic and late repolarization phases. At 12948 nM quinidine, $I_\mathrm{K1}$ maintains a steady outward current around 85--150 nA/$\mu$F from rest, increasing during repolarization to stabilize resting potential at median -89.0 mV (95\% CI: -89.0 to -88.9 mV). Relative to control (peaking near 150 nA/$\mu$F), quinidine slightly reduces late-phase outward $I_\mathrm{K1}$, contributing to mild depolarization and slowed final repolarization, consistent with observed dV/dtMaxRepol of -0.135 mV/ms (95\% CI: -0.172 to -0.106 mV/ms). This supports the overall proarrhythmic profile with high failure rates (96.67\%) at this concentration.
